# Transcrição: 01-Story- The Blood Draw

- **Fonte:** 01-Story- The Blood Draw
- **Duração:** 07:56
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

It's an early Saturday morning. I'm walking across an empty parking lot towards this enormous edifice of a building. It is poured concrete. It is brown and gray. It is brutalist architecture. It's the kind of building that lets me know that the people of the 1970s hated themselves and hated everything around them. Only terrible people create buildings like this. But it's not the building that has me so upset today. It's what's taking place inside the building. Somewhere through the lobby and up the elevator, down a long hallway, in some small room on the third floor, a person is injecting a needle into another person's arm right now, drawing blood. And that's why I'm here today, a blood draw, a simple medical procedure that has performed millions of times a day all over the country, and yet I am terrified about what's going to happen to me. I'm typically in this situation with my wife. She's holding my hand. We're walking across the parking lot together. She's squeezing it for strength. But today she's not with me because a terrible thing has happened. We've had a baby. Her name is Clara and she's about six months old. And so to wake your wife up on a Saturday morning at seven o'clock and say, honey, can you get out of bed? And can you also wake up the sleeping baby and get the baby out of bed? And can everyone get dressed and piled in the car? And can we all go down to the blood draw place? And can you hold my hand because I'm afraid? I did that on this Saturday morning. And she said no. And that's fine. I understand that she doesn't love me as much as she loves the baby. And so I'm stuck here alone. Typically when my wife is not available, what I'll do is I'll ask the nurse to have someone else come into the room for the blood draw to hold my hand. I don't care who it is. I will take any human being who will give me a little courage and a little strength in situations like this. But since I've had a baby, things have changed. The last time I had a blood draw just a couple months ago, I asked the nurse if someone could come in and she said, you're a father now. You're a grown ass man. You have to learn to have some courage. And I wanted to say, I have courage. I am a brave human being. I go out on cold winter days with nothing but a sweatshirt on. I haven't worn mittens in years. I'm tough. I'm the kind of person who goes through life and I'll run through walls. But this is something different. This is a blood draw. It's a needle. It's scary. And nobody seems to care. And so I entered the building. I pressed the button on the carriage of death and it descends down to me. The doors open. I climb inside and I begin to ascend. The first time I ever went to a blood draw with Alicia, it was at Sloan Kettering in New York. One of my students had cancer and I was doting not ... One of my students had cancer and I was doting not blood but platelets, which is even worse than blood because that's two needles, one in each arm and they basically take everything

## [03:03 - 06:08]

out of you, process it and put it back. It takes like 45 minutes. It's a nightmare. And when we were done and I was in a little room drinking orange juice and eating a cookie, the nurse came in to speak to Alicia and me. She told Alicia, I've been doing this job for 30 years and I've never had a worse patient than your husband. So this is a real thing for me. And so the doors to the elevator open and I walked down that long hallway. I enter a room. It's filled with people here for a blood draw. They're all calm. They're all serene. I hate every one of them. I add my name to a list. I sit down and I wait. I'm not talking over there. Oh. Where am I talking? I was trying to do it. Okay. The door is open to the elevator? Yeah. Okay. I'm like explaining it. Okay. The door is open to the elevator. I walk down that long hall. I enter a room filled with people. Every single one of them is calm. They're reading magazines. They're looking at their phones. I hate every one of them. I can't stand their placid serene faces. I add my name to the list. I sit down and I wait. A few minutes later, a young man opens a door and invites me inside. I go down the hallway into the little room. I sit down in the chair and then I tell him what I tell every nurse or every phlebotomist who's about to draw my blood. I say, listen, I'm sorry, but this is going to be really hard for me and I might make it really hard on you, so I just want to apologize up front. This man who's sort of opening up containers and tearing open packages, everything stops. He stops doing everything that he needs to do. He sits down on a little stool with wheels and he rolls right up to me. He puts his hands on my thighs, looks at me and says, why is this so hard for you? It's the first time anyone has ever asked me that question. So I tell him, when I was 12 years old, I was stung by a bee and I was home alone. By the time the ambulance broke down the door and the paramedics made their way to me, I wasn't breathing and my heart wasn't beating and they used CPR to restore my life. Then I went to the hospital where I was injected with epinephrine to save my life. And for the next week, every evening, that poison that was still in my bloodstream would cause me to react. And every night, my parents would rush me back to the hospital for more shots to counteract the reaction. And then because it was the 1980s, I had to learn to give myself shots in case I was stung again. But there were no auto-injectors back then. It was real needles with sterilized water that I would sit at a dining room table and inject into my arm. And it was all terrible. And ever since then, I've been terrified of needles.

## [06:08 - 07:56]

And this man looks at me and he says, that makes sense to me. He says, what you have is a negative feedback loop. And then he points to his chin. He points to this tiny white line on his chin. And he says, when I was eight years old, my friend's dog bit me in the face. And ever since then, I have been terrified of dogs. He tells me that when he goes to his friend's house, they have to put the dog outside or in another room behind a closed door or he never feels safe. He says it doesn't make any sense. But he understands that fear is real. It's the best blood draw I've ever had in my life. When I'm sitting in my car when we're done, I cry over how good that blood draw was. And so now, when I take my students camping in the fall and a little girl tells me that she's afraid of the dark, I don't shoe her away and tell her to get over it. I understand that fear is real. And when I'm teaching my son how to play baseball and he tells me that he's afraid the ball is going to hit him in the head, I don't tell him to get over it. Just catch the ball. I tell him I understand what it's like to be afraid. And when my peanut allergic daughter tells me that she's afraid to eat something new because she's afraid of what might be inside it, I don't tell her to stop being foolish. I tell her that I understand fear. I know that it's real and that perhaps we can conquer it together. Thank you.
